title: Responding to MHRA Inspection Findings
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A practical QP guide to building an MHRA inspection response: grading findings, CAPA, root cause, ALCOA+ evidence and realistic, defensible timelines.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner of a calm, immaculate pharmaceutical quality office at a glass meeting table, where a gloved hand reviews a crisp inspection report and an open quality manual beside a sleek tablet showing abstract trend charts; cool teal and cyan tones blend into deep navy shadows with a single subtle green accent from a status indicator, lit by soft natural window light, shallow depth of field with gentle bokeh, pristine and authoritative, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: How long do we have to respond to MHRA inspection findings?
faqs.a: Timeframes are set by the MHRA and confirmed in the post-inspection correspondence, and they depend on the severity of the findings; critical deficiencies demand the most urgent attention. Rather than fixating on a fixed number of days, agree a realistic timeline with the inspector and meet it. Submitting on time with strong evidence matters far more than rushing an incomplete reply.

faqs.q: What is the most common reason an MHRA inspection response is rejected?
faqs.a: Weak root cause analysis is the most frequent failing, typically when 'human error' is offered as a cause rather than examining why the system allowed and failed to detect the error. Responses are also rejected when corrections are confused with corrective actions, or when actions are closed without objective evidence of effectiveness. A response built on ICH Q9 risk thinking and ICH Q10 system governance is far more credible.

faqs.q: Should the Qualified Person sign off the inspection response?
faqs.a: The response should be coordinated by Quality and endorsed by senior management, with the QP, RP or RPi closely involved in anything affecting batch certification, product release, distribution or importation. Their oversight demonstrates that decisions about product impact and ongoing control have proper accountability. Visible senior and QP ownership also reassures the MHRA that findings are being treated as a priority.
